facts_disclosed: Switched to muay thai, 4x/week Khalifa City, Kru Somchai, 350 dirhams/month

turns.content: what should you eat before a high intensity workout?? like we're talking serious sweating for 90 minutes. I just started this new thing and I keep almost passing out lol

Almost passing out is a sign you're either underfueled, dehydrated, or both. For a 90-minute high-intensity session, you want to eat about 1.5-2 hours before. A good pre-workout meal should be:

- **Moderate carbs** for energy (oats, rice, banana, toast)
- **Some protein** to sustain you (eggs, yogurt, chicken)
- **Low fat/fiber** so it digests fast and doesn't sit heavy

Something like a banana with peanut butter on toast, or rice with chicken and a little veg. What time do you usually train, and what are you eating before?

I train at 7pm usually so I'm coming straight from work. and honestly?? I've been eating nothing because I don't want to feel heavy during class. which is clearly backfiring because last Tuesday I got dizzy during pad work and had to sit down for 5 minutes. embarrassing wallah

Yeah, training on empty for 90 minutes of intense work is a recipe for exactly what happened. Your blood sugar crashes and your body has nothing to fuel the effort. No wonder you're getting dizzy.

Since you're going straight from work at 7pm, try having a snack around 5-5:30pm at the office:

- A banana and a handful of dates
- A small wrap with chicken or hummus
- Greek yogurt with some granola
- Even a protein bar in a pinch

Small enough that you won't feel heavy, but enough to keep your blood sugar stable through the session. What kind of training is this — CrossFit, boxing, something else?

muay thai!! I dropped pilates and started muay thai like 3 weeks ago and wallah I am completely obsessed. I go 4 times a week at this gym in Khalifa City. my trainer is this Thai guy Kru Somchai and he is INTENSE. like no breaks, no excuses, just work. I've never been pushed like this in my life and I love it??

That's a huge shift from Pilates — no wonder your body's adjusting! Muay Thai is one of the most demanding martial arts out there. The pad work alone is exhausting, plus clinching, bag work, conditioning... 4 times a week with an intense trainer is serious commitment.

The good news is your body will adapt fast if you fuel it properly. With that frequency and intensity, nutrition becomes non-negotiable. The dizziness will stop once you start eating before training.

How are you feeling the day after sessions? Any muscle soreness, fatigue?

EVERYTHING HURTS lmao. my shins are destroyed, my shoulders are constantly sore, and I found a bruise on my arm yesterday that I genuinely don't remember getting. but ngl I feel amazing?? like mentally I feel like a completely different person. the confidence is insane. I used to be scared of everything and now I'm throwing elbows at pads and screaming. it's so good for my brain

The mental transformation from martial arts is no joke — there's solid research showing it builds confidence, stress resilience, and even reduces anxiety. The controlled aggression is incredibly therapeutic, especially if you tend to hold things in during the day.

For the soreness and recovery:

- **Protein within 30 minutes** after training — a shake, chocolate milk, or even a chicken shawarma on the way home
- **Magnesium supplements** before bed can help with muscle recovery and sleep
- **Shin conditioning** takes time — your shins will toughen up over the next 2-3 months. In the meantime, arnica gel on the bruises helps
- **Epsom salt baths** on your rest days

How much is the gym? Some Muay Thai places in the UAE also offer open mat sessions where you can drill at a lighter pace — could help on recovery days.

it's 350 dirhams a month which is a bit more than what I was paying before but honestly?? so worth it. I would pay double. Kru Somchai has been doing this for like 20 years, he fought in Thailand professionally. the technique he teaches is so precise. I mean even the way he corrects your stance — he'll move your foot like 2 centimeters and suddenly everything feels different

350 for 4x/week with a trainer who has professional fight experience is genuinely great value. Good Thai trainers are meticulous about stance and technique because in muay thai, power comes from positioning, not muscle. That 2-centimeter foot adjustment probably changed your entire weight distribution and strike angle.

The fact that he's detail-oriented with fundamentals is a really good sign — it means he's building your technique properly rather than just letting you swing at pads for cardio. A lot of gyms do the latter.

Are you interested in eventually sparring, or is this more for fitness and the mental benefits?

he says I'll start light sparring in about a month once my fundamentals are solid and tbh I'm nervous but also excited?? like the idea of actually fighting someone even controlled is terrifying but that's kind of the point. I want to stop being scared of things. I mean I spend all day at work feeling like I'm not good enough and then I go to muay thai and I feel powerful. it's the best thing I've done for myself in so long

That's a great mindset. Light sparring is very controlled — it's about applying technique at low intensity, not hurting each other. Most people find it less scary than they expected once they're actually doing it. And the fact that your trainer is waiting until your fundamentals are solid before letting you spar shows he takes your development seriously.

The confidence transfer you're describing — from the gym to daily life — is one of the most valuable things martial arts offers. When you know you can handle physical discomfort and stay composed under pressure on the mats, workplace stress starts to feel smaller.

Just make sure you're eating before class. The dizzy-during-pad-work thing won't just fix itself. Dates and a banana at 5:30, minimum.

okay okay I hear you!! I'm literally putting a reminder on my phone rn. 5:30 snack alarm. I'll get some dates and keep them in my desk drawer. also should I be drinking something specific during class? I just bring a regular water bottle

Water is fine for most sessions, but for 90 minutes of intense sweating you're losing a lot of electrolytes — sodium, potassium, magnesium. You can either:

- Add a pinch of salt and some lemon to your water bottle
- Get electrolyte tablets (brands like Hydralyte or even ORS sachets from the pharmacy work great)
- Coconut water is a natural option if you like the taste

The salt is the most important one. When you sweat that much and only drink plain water, you dilute your electrolytes further, which can actually make the dizziness worse. Try the salt-and-lemon water at your next session and see how you feel.

ooh the salt and lemon thing is so easy I can do that right now. okay yalla I actually need to leave for class in 20 minutes so good timing lol. thanks for ALL of this — the food advice, the recovery stuff, everything. I feel like I have an actual plan now instead of just showing up and dying!!

Ha — "showing up and dying" is an approach, just not a sustainable one! You've got the plan now: snack at 5:30, salt-lemon water during class, protein after. Your body will catch up to your enthusiasm in no time. Go throw some elbows tonight.
